root_text.an9.7:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati gijjhakūṭe pabbate.
root_text.an9.7:1.2: Atha kho sutavā paribbājako yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavatā saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.an9.7:1.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho sutavā paribbājako bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.an9.7:2.1: “Ekamidāhaṁ, bhante, samayaṁ bhagavā idheva rājagahe viharāmi giribbaje.
root_text.an9.7:2.2: Tatra me, bhante, bhagavato sammukhā sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ:
root_text.an9.7:2.3: ‘yo so, sutavā, bhikkhu arahaṁ khīṇāsavo vusitavā katakaraṇīyo ohitabhāro anuppattasadattho parikkhīṇabhavasaṁyojano sammadaññāvimutto, abhabbo so pañca ṭhānāni ajjhācarituṁ—
root_text.an9.7:2.4: abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu sañcicca pāṇaṁ jīvitā voropetuṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu adinnaṁ theyyasaṅkhātaṁ ādātuṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu methunaṁ dhammaṁ paṭisevituṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu sampajānamusā bhāsituṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu sannidhikārakaṁ kāme paribhuñjituṁ seyyathāpi pubbe agāriyabhūto’ti.
root_text.an9.7:2.5: Kacci metaṁ, bhante, bhagavato sussutaṁ suggahitaṁ sumanasikataṁ sūpadhāritan”ti?
root_text.an9.7:3.1: “Taggha te etaṁ, sutavā, sussutaṁ suggahitaṁ sumanasikataṁ sūpadhāritaṁ.
root_text.an9.7:3.2: Pubbe cāhaṁ, sutavā, etarahi ca evaṁ vadāmi:
root_text.an9.7:3.3: ‘yo so bhikkhu arahaṁ khīṇāsavo vusitavā katakaraṇīyo ohitabhāro anuppattasadattho parikkhīṇabhavasaṁyojano sammadaññāvimutto, abhabbo so nava ṭhānāni ajjhācarituṁ—
root_text.an9.7:3.4: abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu sañcicca pāṇaṁ jīvitā voropetuṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu adinnaṁ theyyasaṅkhātaṁ ādātuṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu methunaṁ dhammaṁ paṭisevituṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu sampajānamusā bhāsituṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu sannidhikārakaṁ kāme paribhuñjituṁ seyyathāpi pubbe agāriyabhūto, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu chandāgatiṁ gantuṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu dosāgatiṁ gantuṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu mohāgatiṁ gantuṁ, abhabbo khīṇāsavo bhikkhu bhayāgatiṁ gantuṁ’.
root_text.an9.7:3.5: Pubbe cāhaṁ, sutavā, etarahi ca evaṁ vadāmi:
root_text.an9.7:3.6: ‘yo so bhikkhu arahaṁ khīṇāsavo vusitavā katakaraṇīyo ohitabhāro anuppattasadattho parikkhīṇabhavasaṁyojano sammadaññāvimutto, abhabbo so imāni nava ṭhānāni ajjhācaritun’”ti.

translation_text.an9.7:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the Vulture’s Peak Mountain.
translation_text.an9.7:1.2: Then the wanderer Sutavā went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.an9.7:1.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha:
translation_text.an9.7:2.1: “Sir, this one time the Buddha was staying right here in Rājagaha, the Mountainfold.
translation_text.an9.7:2.2: There I heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha:
translation_text.an9.7:2.3: ‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in five respects.
translation_text.an9.7:2.4: A mendicant with defilements ended can’t deliberately take the life of a living creature, take something with the intention to steal, have sex, tell a deliberate lie, or store up goods for their own enjoyment like they did as a layperson.’
translation_text.an9.7:2.5: I trust I properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered that from the Buddha?”
translation_text.an9.7:3.1: “Indeed, Sutavā, you properly heard, learned, applied the mind, and remembered that.
translation_text.an9.7:3.2: In the past, as today, I say this:
translation_text.an9.7:3.3: ‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in nine respects.
translation_text.an9.7:3.4: A mendicant with defilements ended can’t deliberately take the life of a living creature, take something with the intention to steal, have sex, tell a deliberate lie, or store up goods for their own enjoyment like they did as a layperson. And they can’t make decisions prejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, or cowardice.’
translation_text.an9.7:3.5: In the past, as today, I say this:
translation_text.an9.7:3.6: ‘A mendicant who is perfected—with defilements ended, who has completed the spiritual journey, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, achieved their heart’s goal, utterly ended the fetter of continued existence, and is rightly freed through enlightenment—can’t transgress in these nine respects.’”
